title: White Lines: The Sacred Leaf and the Industrial Drug
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the difference between coca leaf and cocaine and why it matters.
Subject: Episode introduction | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome back to Better Vice Club, Season 3, The Forbidden Leaf. This is Anthony McDonald. So far this season we have seen tobacco, cannabis, and opioids. Today we reach one of the most misunderstood plants in the world, the coca leaf, and the powerful drug humans learned to extract from it, cocaine.

[HOOK] The honest note first, as always. This is a history and policy class, never a how-to, and nothing here is encouragement to use anything. Cocaine is genuinely dangerous, with serious effects on the heart and a high potential for addiction (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2024). If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours: 1-800-662-4357. Now the hook, and it is the heart of the whole episode. The coca leaf and cocaine are not the same thing. Andean peoples have chewed coca leaf, a practice called acullico, for at least 4,000 years, and the effect is mild, similar to coffee, with no documented evidence of harm like cocaine's (Plowman, 1984). Cocaine is what you get when you take that leaf and use industrial chemistry to concentrate one compound from about 1 percent up to nearly 100 percent. The drug is a 19th-century invention. The plant is ancient and sacred.

[TEACH] We read coca through four lenses. Geography asks where the plant grows and why eradication keeps failing. Social studies follows the leaf from sacred plant to global drug, and tells the documented story of the crack and powder cocaine sentencing gap. Economics asks who got rich from a trillion-dollar war and who paid for it. And English language arts studies the language of drug policy, from a famous slogan to the voices fighting to be heard. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold this above all else. The leap from sacred leaf to destructive drug requires industrial processing, the chemical removal of everything that once held the plant inside culture and community. Coca leaf is not cocaine. Keeping that distinction clear is the key to understanding this entire episode.

[CHECK] Quick check. What turns the mild, ancient coca leaf into the dangerous drug cocaine? [Beat] Industrial processing that concentrates one compound from about 1 percent in the leaf up to nearly 100 percent (Plowman, 1984).

## Key Takeaways
- Coca leaf and cocaine are not the same: the leaf is mild and ancient, cocaine is a concentrated 19th-century industrial drug.
- Andean peoples have chewed coca leaf (acullico) for at least 4,000 years with no documented harm like cocaine's (Plowman, 1984).
- This is education, not a how-to or an endorsement; cocaine is genuinely dangerous (National Institute on Drug Abuse, 2024). Help: SAMHSA 1-800-662-4357.
- Better Vice Club reads coca through geography, social studies, economics, and English language arts.

## Sources
- Plowman, T. (1984). The ethnobotany of coca (Erythroxylum spp., Erythroxylaceae). Advances in Economic Botany, 1, 62-111.
- National Institute on Drug Abuse. (2024). Cocaine research. https://nida.nih.gov/research-topics/cocaine

title: The Yungas
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain where coca grows and why production stays in three Andean countries.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we opened the coca episode. Two quick questions.
One: are coca leaf and cocaine the same thing? [Beat] No; the leaf is mild and ancient, cocaine is a concentrated industrial drug (Plowman, 1984).
Two: how long has acullico been practiced? [Beat] At least 4,000 years (Plowman, 1984).

[HOOK] The coca plant is picky about where it lives. It wants a very specific shelf of the Andes mountains, not too high, not too low, and that narrow band has shaped the entire geography of the cocaine trade.

[TEACH] The plant is Erythroxylum coca, and it grows best in the yungas, the warm, wet, forested foothills on the eastern slopes of the Andes, between about 500 and 2,000 meters of elevation (Plowman, 1984). It needs heavy tropical rainfall of 1,500 to 2,000 millimeters a year, acidic well-drained soil, year-round warmth with no frost, and a great deal of hand labor for its several harvests a year. That combination is hard to find. The plant does not move easily to other regions.
So coca production concentrates in just three countries that share this Andean geography: Colombia, with about 67 percent of the world's coca, Peru with about 26 percent, and Bolivia with about 7 percent (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). In 2022, global cultivation reached a record of around 304,000 hectares, more than ever before, despite decades of eradication. Hold onto that fact, because it tells you something important: after billions of dollars spent trying to wipe coca out, there is more of it than there has ever been. The geography is stubborn, and so is the demand.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Picture the long spine of the Andes, and on its warm eastern slopes a narrow green shelf, the yungas, threading through Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia. That shelf is where almost all the world's coca grows, and it has barely moved in 4,000 years.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: coca (Erythroxylum coca) grows in the warm, wet Andean yungas between about 500 and 2,000 meters, which is why production concentrates in Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia, and global cultivation hit a record of about 304,000 hectares in 2022 despite decades of eradication (Plowman, 1984; United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). The geography is fixed and the demand persistent.

[CHECK] Quick check. Which three countries grow almost all the world's coca, and why those three? [Beat] Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia, because they share the Andean yungas geography the plant needs (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- Coca (Erythroxylum coca) grows in the Andean yungas, the warm, wet foothills at 500 to 2,000 meters (Plowman, 1984).
- It needs heavy tropical rain, acidic soil, no frost, and intensive hand labor, so it does not move easily.
- Colombia (about 67%), Peru (about 26%), and Bolivia (about 7%) grow almost all the world's coca (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
- Global cultivation hit a record of about 304,000 hectares in 2022 despite decades of eradication.

## Sources
- Plowman, T. (1984). The ethnobotany of coca (Erythroxylum spp., Erythroxylaceae). Advances in Economic Botany, 1, 62-111.
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org

title: The Balloon Effect
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why crop eradication relocates coca production instead of ending it.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we found coca in the yungas. Two quick questions.
One: which three countries grow almost all the world's coca? [Beat] Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
Two: what happened to global cultivation by 2022 despite eradication? [Beat] It hit a record of about 304,000 hectares (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

[HOOK] Squeeze a balloon in one spot and the air does not disappear. It bulges out somewhere else. That simple image is the single most important lesson in the geography of the drug war, and it has a name: the balloon effect.

[TEACH] The balloon effect is what happens when eradication in one area pushes production to another area rather than eliminating it (Dion & Russler, 2008). The clearest case is Plan Colombia. Starting in 2000, the United States invested more than 10 billion dollars and sprayed millions of hectares with the herbicide glyphosate from the air to destroy coca. Colombia had about 90,000 hectares of coca in 2000. By 2020, after twenty years and ten billion dollars, it had about 204,000 hectares, more than double (Dion & Russler, 2008). And during those same years, when Colombian enforcement pressed hard, cultivation shifted into Peru and Bolivia in response.
The geographic lesson is blunt. Coca follows two maps at once: where it is profitable to grow, and where enforcement is weakest. Attacking the supply, the crop, without reducing demand or offering farmers another way to earn a living, just moves production around. It does not end it. And the spraying did real damage, drifting onto legal crops, waterways, and ecosystems, displacing rural families and poisoning soil and water, until Colombia's own Constitutional Court suspended aerial fumigation in 2015 over the environmental and health harm.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Picture a hand squeezing the coca fields of Colombia, and the production simply bulging outward into Peru and Bolivia, then back again. Ten billion dollars, twenty years, and at the end more coca than at the start. The balloon never popped. It just changed shape.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the balloon effect means eradicating coca in one place pushes production elsewhere, so Plan Colombia's 10 billion dollars and aerial spraying left Colombia with more than double its coca by 2020, because supply-side enforcement without demand reduction or economic alternatives relocates production rather than ending it (Dion & Russler, 2008). Squeeze the balloon, and it bulges.

[CHECK] Quick check. What does the balloon effect demonstrate about crop eradication? [Beat] That destroying the crop in one area pushes production to another rather than eliminating it, as Plan Colombia showed (Dion & Russler, 2008).

## Key Takeaways
- The balloon effect is when eradication pushes drug production to a new area instead of ending it (Dion & Russler, 2008).
- Plan Colombia spent more than 10 billion dollars and sprayed glyphosate, yet Colombia's coca more than doubled by 2020.
- Coca follows where it is profitable and where enforcement is weakest, so supply-side attacks just relocate it.
- The aerial spraying caused environmental and health harm, and Colombia's Constitutional Court suspended it in 2015.

## Sources
- Dion, M. L., & Russler, C. (2008). Eradication efforts, the state, displacement and poverty: Explaining coca cultivation in Colombia during Plan Colombia. Journal of Latin American Studies, 40(3), 399-421.
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org

title: Following the Powder
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can trace the cocaine supply chain and explain where the value and risk fall.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we learned the balloon effect. Two quick questions.
One: what does eradicating coca in one place do? [Beat] It pushes production elsewhere, the balloon effect (Dion & Russler, 2008).
Two: what happened to Colombia's coca despite Plan Colombia? [Beat] It more than doubled by 2020 (Dion & Russler, 2008).

[HOOK] A coca leaf grown on an Andean hillside and a line of powder sold in a distant city are connected by one of the longest, most dangerous supply chains in the world. Following that chain shows you exactly where the money is made and where the risk is dumped.

[TEACH] The chain runs in clear stages (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). It starts with production in Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia. The leaf is then processed in jungle laboratories that reduce its bulk into a compact, high-value form for transport. From there it moves through transit corridors, mostly Central America and Mexico for the U.S. market, and increasingly through the Caribbean and West Africa for Europe. It enters consuming countries across borders and through ports, and finally moves through urban distribution networks down to the street.
Here is the pattern we have seen in every commodity this whole series, now at its most extreme. The farmer at the start earns the least and bears enormous risk. The value multiplies enormously along the chain, and the biggest profits are captured in the middle and near the consuming end, by trafficking organizations, not growers. Even a city like Indianapolis sits on this map: at the crossing of Interstate 65 and Interstate 70, it became a documented Midwest distribution hub, and the crack epidemic of the 1980s and 1990s concentrated in specific near-north and near-east neighborhoods. The supply chain reaches all the way from a hillside in the yungas to a street corner in the Midwest.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Trace the line: a green hillside in the Andes, a hidden jungle lab, a route up through Mexico, a crossing at the U.S. border, then a fan of highways spreading out through hubs like Indianapolis. A second line peels off toward West Africa and Europe. One leaf, a thousand hands, a global chain.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: cocaine follows a long supply chain, production in the Andes, processing in jungle labs, transit through Mexico or West Africa, entry across borders, and urban distribution, where the farmer earns the least and bears the most risk while traffickers capture the value, with hubs like Indianapolis sitting on the distribution map (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). The chain dumps risk at the bottom.

[CHECK] Quick check. Along the cocaine supply chain, who earns the least and bears the most risk? [Beat] The coca farmer at the start; the biggest profits are captured by traffickers in the middle and near the consuming end (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

## Key Takeaways
- The cocaine supply chain runs from Andean production through jungle processing, transit, border entry, and urban distribution (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
- The U.S. market is supplied mostly through Central America and Mexico; Europe increasingly through West Africa.
- As in every commodity this series, the farmer earns the least and bears the most risk while traffickers capture the value.
- Indianapolis, at the crossing of Interstate 65 and 70, is a documented Midwest distribution hub.

## Sources
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org
- Dion, M. L., & Russler, C. (2008). Eradication efforts, the state, displacement and poverty: Explaining coca cultivation in Colombia during Plan Colombia. Journal of Latin American Studies, 40(3), 399-421.

title: Leaf and Powder
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the difference between sacred coca leaf and industrial cocaine, and how the drug emerged.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we followed the powder along its supply chain. Two quick questions.
One: along the cocaine supply chain, who earns the least and bears the most risk? [Beat] The coca farmer at the start; traffickers in the middle and near the consuming end capture the value (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
Two: name one transit route the chain uses to reach its markets. [Beat] Central America and Mexico for the U.S. market, or the Caribbean and West Africa for Europe (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).

[HOOK] There is a leaf chewed in the Andes for thousands of years, and there is a white powder sold on city streets. People treat them as the same thing. They are not. Getting this distinction right is the whole point of this episode, so let us be careful with it.

[TEACH] Start with the leaf. The traditional practice is called acullico, chewing coca leaf with a bit of lime, usually ash or limestone. The leaf holds the cocaine alkaloid at a concentration of about 0.5 to 1 percent (Plowman, 1984). Chewed slowly and absorbed through the gum, it gives a mild stimulation, roughly the equivalent of a strong cup of coffee. It eases hunger and altitude sickness. This practice has been documented in the Andes for at least 4,000 years, held inside communal and sacred protocol, and it carries no documented harm comparable to cocaine (Plowman, 1984).
Now the powder. Cocaine hydrochloride is nearly pure alkaloid, around 98 to 100 percent. It delivers intense, rapid euphoria, with serious effects on the heart and a high potential for addiction. It is not a plant you chew. It is an industrial product, made in a laboratory.
So how did one become the other? In 1860, the German chemist Albert Niemann isolated the cocaine alkaloid from the leaf and named it (Plowman, 1984). That isolation began the industrialization of coca. The plant moved into pharmacies and tonics. In 1886, John Pemberton launched Coca-Cola with coca-leaf extract in the formula, marketed as a brain tonic. Coca was removed from the formula in 1903, though the brand name kept the plant's identity in commercial memory (Pendergrast, 2000).
Here is the key insight. Turning the sacred leaf into the destructive drug required industrial processing that stripped away everything that had held the plant inside culture and governance. The slow absorption, the mild dose, the ceremony, the communal rules, all of it gone. What was left was a concentrated chemical with none of the protocol that had kept the leaf in its place for four thousand years.

[PICTURE] Picture two hands. One holds a green leaf, tucked into the cheek at a high-altitude gathering, passed between neighbors as a greeting. The other holds a vial of white powder made in a hidden lab. Same plant family at the root. But between the two hands sits a chemist's bench and a century of industry, and that bench changed everything.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: coca leaf chewed as acullico holds about 0.5 to 1 percent cocaine alkaloid, gives a mild coffee-like stimulation, and has been practiced for at least 4,000 years inside sacred protocol with no documented comparable harm, while cocaine hydrochloride is a nearly pure, industrially made chemical with intense effects and high addiction potential (Plowman, 1984). Niemann isolated the alkaloid in 1860, and Coca-Cola carried coca-leaf extract from 1886 until 1903 (Pendergrast, 2000). The leap from leaf to powder required industrial processing that stripped away the culture and governance around the plant.

[CHECK] Quick check. What did industrial processing strip away when it turned the leaf into the powder? [Beat] Everything that had held the plant inside culture and governance, the slow absorption, the mild dose, and the communal and sacred protocol, leaving a concentrated chemical (Plowman, 1984).

## Key Takeaways
- Coca leaf chewed as acullico holds about 0.5 to 1 percent cocaine alkaloid and gives a mild stimulation similar to coffee (Plowman, 1984).
- Cocaine hydrochloride is nearly pure alkaloid, an industrial product with intense effects and high addiction potential, not the same as the leaf.
- Albert Niemann isolated the cocaine alkaloid in 1860, beginning industrialization; Coca-Cola carried coca-leaf extract from 1886 until coca was removed in 1903 (Plowman, 1984; Pendergrast, 2000).
- Turning the sacred leaf into the drug required industrial processing that stripped away the culture and governance that had held the plant in place for 4,000 years.

## Sources
- Plowman, T. (1984). The ethnobotany of coca (Erythroxylum spp., Erythroxylaceae). Advances in Economic Botany, 1, 62-111.
- Pendergrast, M. (2000). For God, country and Coca-Cola (2nd ed.). Basic Books.
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org

title: The 100 to 1
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the crack and powder cocaine sentencing disparity and its documented racial outcomes.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we sorted the leaf from the powder. Two quick questions.
One: what is the difference between acullico and cocaine? [Beat] Acullico is chewing coca leaf at about 0.5 to 1 percent alkaloid for a mild coffee-like stimulation; cocaine is a nearly pure, industrially made chemical with intense effects and high addiction potential (Plowman, 1984).
Two: what did industrial processing strip away? [Beat] Everything that had held the plant inside culture and governance, leaving a concentrated chemical (Plowman, 1984).

[HOOK] Two people hold the same molecule. One holds it as powder, one holds it cooked into crack. Under federal law for decades, those two people faced wildly different futures. Not because the chemical was different. Because of how it was processed and who tended to be holding it.

[TEACH] In 1986, Congress passed the Anti-Drug Abuse Act. It set a five-year mandatory minimum prison sentence for 5 grams of crack cocaine. To trigger that same five-year sentence with powder cocaine, you needed 500 grams (Alexander, 2010). Five grams against 500. That is a 100 to 1 ratio.
Now hold the chemistry next to the law. Crack and powder cocaine are the same molecule, cocaine, processed differently. Crack is cocaine mixed with baking soda and heated into a smokable form with a faster onset. Same drug. The law treated one gram of it as the equal of one hundred grams of the other (Alexander, 2010).
Here is the documented outcome, and we present it plainly because it is well sourced. Black Americans made up roughly 80 percent of those sentenced under federal crack cocaine laws. White Americans were the majority of powder cocaine users and sellers (Alexander, 2010). So the same molecule brought dramatically different consequences in different communities. The crack defendant went to federal prison; the powder user, holding the same drug in a different form, often did not face the same mandatory minimum. That is the heart of it: one molecule, two sets of rules, two sets of communities.
The law has been partly corrected. The Fair Sentencing Act of 2010 lowered the ratio from 100 to 1 down to 18 to 1. The First Step Act of 2018 made that change retroactive, so some people sentenced under the old rule could petition for release (Alexander, 2010). That is real progress. But notice what remains. An 18 to 1 disparity still sits in federal law. Same molecule, still treated unequally, just by a smaller multiplier.

[PICTURE] Picture a courthouse scale, the kind meant to weigh evidence evenly. On one side, 5 grams of crack. On the other, 500 grams of powder. The law says those two pans balance, that they deserve the same five years. Look at the scale and you can see the distortion built right into the metal. And when the smaller pan kept landing in one set of neighborhoods and the larger pan in another, the scale stopped measuring the drug and started sorting people.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the 1986 Anti-Drug Abuse Act required 5 grams of crack but 500 grams of powder to trigger the same five-year mandatory minimum, a 100 to 1 ratio for the same molecule processed differently, and Black Americans were roughly 80 percent of those sentenced under federal crack laws while white Americans were the majority of powder users and sellers (Alexander, 2010). The 2010 Fair Sentencing Act cut the ratio to 18 to 1 and the 2018 First Step Act made it retroactive, but an 18 to 1 disparity still remains.

[CHECK] Quick check. What was the documented racial outcome of the 100 to 1 disparity? [Beat] Black Americans made up roughly 80 percent of those sentenced under federal crack laws while white Americans were the majority of powder users and sellers, so the same molecule brought dramatically different consequences in different communities (Alexander, 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- The 1986 Anti-Drug Abuse Act set a five-year mandatory minimum for 5 grams of crack but 500 grams of powder, a 100 to 1 ratio (Alexander, 2010).
- Crack and powder are the same molecule, cocaine, processed differently; crack is made smokable with baking soda.
- Black Americans were roughly 80 percent of those sentenced under federal crack laws while white Americans were the majority of powder users and sellers (Alexander, 2010).
- The 2010 Fair Sentencing Act cut the ratio to 18 to 1 and the 2018 First Step Act made it retroactive, but an 18 to 1 disparity still remains.

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.

title: Coca Si, Cocaina No
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain Bolivia's framework distinguishing the traditional plant from the industrial drug.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at the 100 to 1 disparity. Two quick questions.
One: what was the 1986 sentencing ratio between crack and powder cocaine? [Beat] 100 to 1; 5 grams of crack triggered the same five-year mandatory minimum as 500 grams of powder (Alexander, 2010).
Two: what was the documented racial outcome? [Beat] Black Americans were roughly 80 percent of those sentenced under federal crack laws while white Americans were the majority of powder users and sellers (Alexander, 2010).

[HOOK] Most of the world treats the coca leaf and cocaine as one banned thing. One country built a policy on the opposite idea, that the sacred leaf and the industrial drug are not the same and should not share a fate. That country is Bolivia, and its slogan is "coca si, cocaina no." Coca yes, cocaine no.

[TEACH] Here is what the policy actually does. Bolivia legalized traditional coca cultivation, allowing each family to grow up to one cato, about 1,600 square meters (Grisaffi, 2019). That growing is regulated by cocalero cooperatives, the coca growers' own community organizations, rather than by outside force. And the policy permits legal markets for the leaf itself: coca tea, traditional chewing, and products like food and toothpaste (Grisaffi, 2019). The idea is to keep the leaf legal and inside community governance while keeping cocaine illegal.
Now the political story, because this did not come from nowhere. In 2005, Bolivia elected Evo Morales, a former leader of the coca-growers' union, as the country's first Indigenous president (Grisaffi, 2019). Under his government, Bolivia expelled the U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration in 2008. Then, in 2012, Bolivia negotiated a carve-out in the UN drug treaties that recognized coca leaf chewing's distinct status, an international acknowledgment that the leaf is not simply cocaine (Grisaffi, 2019).
And here is the genuine debate, because this is contested and you should hear both sides. Supporters argue that criminalizing the leaf itself, the leaf used in acullico for thousands of years, is cultural colonialism against a sacred Indigenous practice. To them, controlling cocaine is one thing, but banning the plant a culture holds sacred is an attack on Indigenous sovereignty (Grisaffi, 2019). Critics argue the other way: that a legal coca market creates cover, that some legally grown leaf can be diverted into cocaine processing, so the legal crop can quietly feed the illegal drug. Both of those concerns are real. Bolivia's framework is an attempt to honor the first without ignoring the second, and whether it holds is a live question.

[PICTURE] Picture a market stall in a Bolivian town. Sacks of green coca leaf for tea and chewing, a box of coca-leaf toothpaste, a vendor with a license from a growers' cooperative. Now picture a foreign official who has spent decades treating that same leaf as contraband, standing at the edge of the stall, unsure whether he is looking at a cultural tradition or a loophole. That uncertainty, standing right there in the market, is the whole debate.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Bolivia's "coca si, cocaina no" framework legalized traditional coca cultivation up to one cato (about 1,600 square meters) per family, regulated through cocalero cooperatives, and permits markets for coca tea, chewing, and products like food and toothpaste; Evo Morales, a former coca-growers' union leader, became Bolivia's first Indigenous president in 2005, the country expelled the U.S. DEA in 2008, and it won a UN treaty carve-out recognizing coca leaf in 2012 (Grisaffi, 2019). Supporters call criminalizing the leaf cultural colonialism; critics say the legal market can enable cocaine processing.

[CHECK] Quick check. What is the genuine debate over Bolivia's framework? [Beat] Supporters argue that criminalizing the sacred leaf itself is cultural colonialism against an Indigenous practice, while critics argue the legal coca market can enable cocaine processing (Grisaffi, 2019).

## Key Takeaways
- Bolivia's "coca si, cocaina no" policy legalized traditional coca cultivation up to one cato (about 1,600 square meters) per family, regulated through cocalero cooperatives (Grisaffi, 2019).
- The policy permits legal markets for coca tea, traditional chewing, and products like food and toothpaste, while keeping cocaine illegal.
- Evo Morales, a former coca-growers' union leader, became Bolivia's first Indigenous president in 2005; Bolivia expelled the U.S. DEA in 2008 and won a UN treaty carve-out for coca leaf in 2012 (Grisaffi, 2019).
- Supporters argue that criminalizing the leaf is cultural colonialism against a sacred Indigenous practice; critics argue the legal market can enable cocaine processing.

## Sources
- Grisaffi, T. (2019). Coca yes, cocaine no: How Bolivia's coca growers reshaped democracy. Duke University Press.
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.

title: The Trillion-Dollar War
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how criminalization creates economic power for trafficking organizations.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we sat with Bolivia's "coca si, cocaina no." Two quick questions.
One: what does the "coca si" policy allow? [Beat] It allows the legal growing, sale, and traditional use of the coca leaf, while keeping cocaine illegal (Grisaffi, 2019).
Two: give one argument for or against that policy. [Beat] For: it protects a traditional Andean plant and gives farmers a legal income. Against: critics say a legal leaf market can leak into cocaine production (Grisaffi, 2019).

[HOOK] Here is a number that should stop you. The world spends more than one hundred billion dollars a year on cocaine. And the United States has spent more than one trillion dollars fighting the War on Drugs since nineteen seventy-one. Two of the largest money flows in modern history, pointed straight at each other, and neither one is winning. Today we follow that money and ask a hard question. What if the war itself is what makes the trade so rich?

[TEACH] Start with the scale. The global cocaine market runs at more than one hundred billion dollars every year (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). Against it sits more than one trillion dollars of U.S. War on Drugs spending since nineteen seventy-one. Now here is the part most people miss. Making cocaine illegal does not just fail to shrink that market. It is the reason the market is worth so much.
Think about three forces working together. First, the price premium. When you ban something people still want, you do not lower the price. You raise it. The cost to grow coca and turn it into cocaine in Colombia is tiny. The street price in a U.S. city is enormous. The gap between those two numbers is a risk premium, the extra money buyers pay because the product is illegal and dangerous to move. And that premium does not go to the farmer. It is captured by the trafficking organizations who take the risk.
Second, the market monopoly. In a legal market, sellers compete, and competition pushes prices down. An illegal market cannot have open competition. It has cartels instead. Cartels capture the economic rent that fair competition would have spread around.
Third, violence as enforcement. In a legal business, if someone breaks a deal, you take them to court. In an illegal business, there are no contracts and no courts. So violence becomes the way to enforce deals and hold territory. Violence is just a cost of doing illegal business.
Now put a face on it. At his peak, Pablo Escobar's Medellin cartel supplied about eighty percent of the cocaine in the United States, and his net worth reached around thirty billion dollars (Bowden, 2001). Thirty billion dollars. That fortune was not built on growing a plant. It was built entirely on the markup that criminalization created, because cocaine's production cost in Colombia was a tiny fraction of its street price in the United States (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). The ban made the gap. Escobar pocketed the gap.

[PICTURE] Picture a coca leaf worth almost nothing on an Andean hillside. Now picture the same product, processed and smuggled, selling for a fortune on a city street. Draw a line between those two prices, and watch it stretch wider and wider the more illegal and dangerous the path becomes. That stretched line is the risk premium. Stand Escobar's thirty billion dollars on top of it, and you can see the whole tower was built on one thing: the gap the ban created.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: criminalization creates economic power for trafficking organizations through the price premium, banning cocaine makes it cost far more than production alone, and that risk premium goes to traffickers, not farmers, while the lack of legal contracts makes violence the way to enforce deals; Pablo Escobar built a fortune of around thirty billion dollars on that markup, supplying about eighty percent of U.S. cocaine (Bowden, 2001; United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). The war made the trade rich.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does making cocaine illegal make trafficking organizations powerful? [Beat] Because the ban creates a price premium, cocaine costs far more than production alone, and that extra money is captured by traffickers, who use violence instead of courts to enforce their deals (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023; Bowden, 2001).

## Key Takeaways
- The global cocaine market runs at more than one hundred billion dollars a year, against more than one trillion dollars of U.S. War on Drugs spending since nineteen seventy-one (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
- Criminalization creates a price premium that goes to traffickers, not farmers, because production cost is a tiny fraction of street price.
- Illegal markets have cartels instead of competition, and violence replaces legal contracts for enforcement.
- Pablo Escobar supplied about eighty percent of U.S. cocaine and was worth around thirty billion dollars, a fortune built on the markup the ban created (Bowden, 2001).

## Sources
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org
- Bowden, M. (2001). Killing Pablo: The hunt for the world's greatest outlaw. Atlantic Monthly Press.

title: The Cost of the Disparity
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain the economic consequences of the crack and powder sentencing disparity on communities.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we counted the trillion-dollar war. Two quick questions.
One: what is the price premium? [Beat] It is the extra money buyers pay because cocaine is illegal, the gap between its tiny production cost and its high street price, and it goes to traffickers (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023).
Two: roughly what was Escobar's peak net worth? [Beat] Around thirty billion dollars (Bowden, 2001).

[HOOK] We have been counting the money the war made for cartels. Now turn the camera around and count the money the war cost at home. Not on the supply side, in the Andes, but right here, in American neighborhoods. The crack and powder sentencing disparity did not just send people to prison. It pulled wealth out of whole communities, and the bill is still being paid today.

[TEACH] Start with the direct cost, the easy one to see. The United States incarcerates roughly four hundred thousand people for drug offenses, and locking up one person costs about thirty-five thousand to forty thousand dollars every year (Alexander, 2010). Multiply that out and you are looking at billions of dollars a year just to hold people behind bars. But the direct cost is only the part you can see on a budget line.
The deeper damage is in the indirect costs, and economic research has documented them carefully (Alexander, 2010). Think about what happens when you remove working-age adults from a neighborhood. You shrink the community's labor force. You cut its household income. You cut its consumer spending, the money that keeps local stores and jobs alive. The neighborhood gets economically thinner every time someone is taken away.
And it does not end when the sentence ends. A criminal record follows a person home. It restricts access to housing, to jobs, to student loans. Each of those is a door to building a future, and the record quietly closes them, which cuts that person's lifetime earnings for years to come. On top of that, felony disenfranchisement strips voting rights, so a community that is already losing income also loses political power, the very power it would need to change the policy hurting it.
Now connect this to the bigger picture, the racial wealth gap. Mass incarceration, driven substantially by the War on Drugs, hit Black communities hardest during the nineteen eighties to the two thousands (Alexander, 2010). And here is the cruel timing. Those same decades were a period of broad economic growth across the United States, a time when families across the country were building wealth, buying homes, and passing it on. The communities hit hardest by crack enforcement were locked out of that growth instead of sharing in it. That is why the neighborhoods most affected by crack enforcement are among the most economically distressed places in American cities today. The wealth that should have been built was disrupted, person by person, year by year.

[PICTURE] Picture a single block during the growth years. Across the country, families are gaining ground, building savings, buying homes. But on this block, working-age adults keep disappearing into the system, and the ones who return come back with records that lock the doors to housing and jobs. The stores close. The voting rolls shrink. While the rest of the economy rises, this block is held down, and the gap between it and everywhere else grows wider with every year.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the crack and powder disparity carries a direct cost, roughly four hundred thousand people incarcerated for drug offenses at about thirty-five thousand to forty thousand dollars each per year, plus heavy indirect costs, lost labor force, income, and spending, criminal records that cut lifetime earnings, and disenfranchisement that cuts political power, all of which disrupted wealth-building in Black communities during a period of broad U.S. economic growth (Alexander, 2010). The bill is still being paid.

[CHECK] Quick check. Beyond the prison budget, name one indirect economic cost of the disparity. [Beat] Any of these: removing working-age adults shrinks a community's labor force, income, and spending; criminal records restrict housing, jobs, and student loans and cut lifetime earnings; felony disenfranchisement cuts political power (Alexander, 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- The United States incarcerates roughly four hundred thousand people for drug offenses at about thirty-five thousand to forty thousand dollars each per year (Alexander, 2010).
- Removing working-age adults shrinks a community's labor force, income, and consumer spending.
- Criminal records restrict housing, jobs, and student loans, cutting lifetime earnings, and felony disenfranchisement cuts political power.
- Mass incarceration disrupted wealth-building in Black communities during the nineteen eighties to two thousands, a period of broad U.S. economic growth, deepening the racial wealth gap (Alexander, 2010).

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.

title: Bolivia's Bet
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can compare Bolivia's legal coca model with U.S.-funded eradication.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we counted the cost of the disparity. Two quick questions.
One: roughly what does it cost to incarcerate one person for a year? [Beat] About thirty-five thousand to forty thousand dollars (Alexander, 2010).
Two: name one indirect cost of mass incarceration. [Beat] Any of these: lost labor force, income, and spending; criminal records that cut lifetime earnings; or disenfranchisement that cuts political power (Alexander, 2010).

[HOOK] Two countries faced the same plant and chose opposite bets. One tried to wipe coca off the map by force, funded with billions of U.S. dollars. The other made the leaf legal and pointed its enforcement at cocaine instead. Today we put those two bets side by side and ask which one actually delivered for farmers, for communities, and for the budget.

[TEACH] Start with Bolivia's bet. Bolivia built a legal coca model, run through domestic regulation and through cocalero cooperatives, the farmer-run groups that manage how much coca gets grown (Grisaffi, 2019). Inside that system, a coca farmer earns a legal income of about one thousand to three thousand dollars a year. That is not a fortune, but it is steady, it is legal, and it keeps families on their land. Added up, that legal coca economy contributes roughly three hundred fifty million dollars a year to Bolivia's economy (Grisaffi, 2019). And the country keeps its enforcement focused on cocaine, the processed drug, rather than on the leaf itself. The bet is simple: keep the plant legal, keep communities stable, and chase the drug.
Now the other bet, U.S.-funded eradication, with Plan Colombia as the clearest example. This approach cost more than ten billion dollars. The strategy was force: destroy the crops in the field. But destroying crops also displaced families, who frequently returned to coca because it was the one income they could count on. And after all that money and disruption, the result was blunt. Cocaine production did not drop significantly. On top of that, the eradication carried net-negative environmental costs, harm to the land that outweighed the gains. Billions spent, families uprooted, and the supply largely intact.
Now, hold this comparison honestly, because it is not a clean win. Bolivia's model has real critics, and their concern is serious. They argue that a legal coca market can leak into cocaine production, that some of the legal leaf gets diverted toward the illegal drug. That is a genuine risk, and the model lives or dies on how well the leaf can be kept separate from the powder. So the fair summary is this. Bolivia's bet kept farmers earning and communities stable at low cost, while eradication spent billions and largely failed at its stated goal, but Bolivia's bet carries a leakage risk that honest people still debate.

[PICTURE] Picture two hillsides. On the first, a Bolivian farmer tends a legal coca plot, earning a steady wage, his cooperative deciding how much to grow, the village quiet and intact. On the second, a Colombian field is being destroyed under a program that cost more than ten billion dollars, the family driven off, then drifting back months later to plant coca again because nothing else paid. Same plant, two very different bets, and very different bills at the bottom.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Bolivia's legal coca model, run through domestic regulation and cocalero cooperatives, gives farmers about one thousand to three thousand dollars a year, adds roughly three hundred fifty million dollars a year to the economy, and keeps communities stable while targeting cocaine not the leaf; U.S.-funded eradication like Plan Colombia cost more than ten billion dollars, displaced families who often returned to coca, did not significantly cut production, and carried net-negative environmental costs, though critics warn a legal coca market can leak into cocaine (Grisaffi, 2019). Two bets, two bills.

[CHECK] Quick check. How does Bolivia's legal coca model compare with U.S.-funded eradication on cost and outcome? [Beat] Bolivia keeps farmers earning about one thousand to three thousand dollars a year and communities stable at low cost while targeting cocaine, whereas eradication spent more than ten billion dollars, displaced families, and did not significantly reduce production, though critics warn the legal leaf can leak into cocaine (Grisaffi, 2019).

## Key Takeaways
- Bolivia's legal coca model gives farmers a legal income of about one thousand to three thousand dollars a year and adds roughly three hundred fifty million dollars a year to its economy (Grisaffi, 2019).
- Bolivia runs the model through domestic regulation and cocalero cooperatives, keeping enforcement on cocaine rather than the leaf.
- Plan Colombia cost more than ten billion dollars, displaced families who often returned to coca, did not significantly reduce production, and carried net-negative environmental costs.
- The fair counterpoint: critics argue a legal coca market can leak into cocaine production (Grisaffi, 2019).

## Sources
- Grisaffi, T. (2019). Coca yes, cocaine no: How Bolivia's coca growers reshaped democracy. Duke University Press.

title: Just Say No
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how a drug-policy frame makes some things visible and others invisible.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at Bolivia's bet on a legal coca model. Two quick questions.
One: name one outcome of Bolivia's legal coca model. [Beat] Legal traditional coca contributes about 350 million dollars a year to Bolivia's economy, and farmers earn a modest income that is comparable to or better than the alternative-crop programs offered as substitutes (Grisaffi, 2019).
Two: name one outcome of Plan Colombia's eradication push. [Beat] Coca cultivation increased rather than ended, the balloon effect, with Colombia's coca more than doubling by 2020 (Dion & Russler, 2008).

[HOOK] In 1986, Nancy Reagan launched a three-word anti-drug campaign that became the defining slogan of American drug policy for a decade: "Just Say No." The phrase is so simple it later became a punchline. But examining why it became a punchline tells you almost everything about how drug-policy language works.

[TEACH] Start with the three words. "Just" implies the problem is simple. "Say" frames the whole thing as a speech act, as if refusal were only a matter of speaking. "No" is the single word you are supposed to say. Put together, the slogan makes one argument: drug use is a simple problem with a simple solution that takes only an act of will.

That is reductive framing, simplifying a complex issue down to a single dimension. And here is the key media-literacy move. A frame is not neutral. It makes some evidence visible and other evidence invisible. The "Just Say No" frame makes individual willpower visible and makes everything else invisible: poverty, trauma, lack of opportunity, peer environment, mental health conditions, and physical dependence. By treating drug use as a private moral choice, the frame quietly implies a policy: if using is a personal failure, then punishment is the answer (Reinarman & Levine, 1997).

Now notice that this is only one frame among several, and each one lights up different evidence and points to a different policy. The individual-moral-failure frame treats use as a choice and implies punishment. The public-health frame treats addiction as a health condition driven by social conditions and implies treatment, prevention, and harm reduction. The racial-justice frame treats enforcement as racial control dressed in neutral language and implies sentencing reform and community investment. The Indigenous-sovereignty frame treats criminalizing a traditional plant as cultural colonialism and implies recognizing the difference between the plant and the processed drug. The economic-rational-choice frame treats use and dealing as responses to incentives and implies economic alternatives.

The point of laying them side by side is not to crown a winner. It is to notice the move itself. Every one of these frames makes some facts easy to see and pushes other facts into the dark, and every one of them carries a policy inside it. So the way you read drug-policy language critically is to ask two questions of any statement: which frame is this using, and who gains when that frame is the one we accept?

[PICTURE] Picture a single spotlight in a dark room. Wherever the beam points, you see clearly. Everything outside the beam disappears. "Just Say No" is a spotlight aimed at one thing, individual willpower, while poverty, trauma, and the whole system stand in the dark just off to the side. The public-health frame, the racial-justice frame, the Indigenous-sovereignty frame, and the economic frame are each just the same spotlight pointed somewhere else. Reading critically means asking who is holding the light.

[REMEMBER] The skill to carry out: a frame is a choice about what to make visible. "Just Say No" applies reductive framing, simplifying drug use to a single dimension of individual moral choice, which makes the systemic causes invisible and implies punishment as the policy (Reinarman & Levine, 1997). Because each competing frame, individual-moral-failure, public-health, racial-justice, Indigenous-sovereignty, and economic-rational-choice, lights up different evidence and implies a different policy, you read drug-policy language critically by asking which frame is in use and who gains from it.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does asking "which frame is this?" help you read drug-policy language critically? [Beat] Because every frame makes some evidence visible and other evidence invisible, and each one implies a different policy, so naming the frame, and asking who gains from it, exposes the choice hidden inside the language (Reinarman & Levine, 1997).

## Key Takeaways
- Reductive framing simplifies a complex issue to a single dimension; "Just Say No" reduces drug use to individual willpower (Reinarman & Levine, 1997).
- A frame makes some evidence visible and other evidence invisible, and it carries a policy inside it.
- Five competing frames, individual-moral-failure, public-health, racial-justice, Indigenous-sovereignty, and economic-rational-choice, each light up different facts and imply different policies.
- Reading drug-policy language critically means asking which frame is in use and who gains from it.

## Sources
- Reinarman, C., & Levine, H. G. (1997). Crack in America: Demon drugs and social justice. University of California Press.
documents: [{"title":"The Just Say No Campaign (1986)","description":"Background on Nancy Reagan's anti-drug campaign, a case study in reductive political messaging. Useful for the media-literacy lessons.","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Just_Say_No","source_url":"Reference summary"}]

title: Whose Voice
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how who tells a story shapes what the story can show.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we analyzed "Just Say No." Two quick questions.
One: what is reductive framing? [Beat] Simplifying a complex issue down to a single dimension, the way "Just Say No" reduces drug use to individual willpower (Reinarman & Levine, 1997).
Two: name two competing drug-policy frames. [Beat] Any two of these: individual-moral-failure, public-health, racial-justice, Indigenous-sovereignty, and economic-rational-choice (Reinarman & Levine, 1997).

[HOOK] Last time we asked which frame a story uses. Today we ask a different question, one step further back: who is telling it? Because the teller shapes the story. Two people can report the same facts and produce two different truths, depending on where they stand.

[TEACH] Start in 1609. Inca Garcilaso de la Vega, the son of an Inca princess and a Spanish captain, published Comentarios Reales de los Incas, a history of the Inca empire and Andean culture. It is one of the earliest hybrid texts of the colonial period: written in Spanish, shaped by European literary conventions, but drawing on Andean oral tradition and direct knowledge of the culture. His account of coca is documentary and respectful. He explains the plant's sacred significance and its social functions, and he states plainly that its traditional uses involve no harm (Garcilaso de la Vega, 1609).

Here is what makes the text worth studying. Garcilaso is carrying Andean Indigenous knowledge across a border, into a European language and form, for a European audience. Something is gained: the knowledge is preserved at the moment of colonial encounter. Something is also lost: when oral Andean knowledge is translated into European conventions, its original cultural context is partly flattened to fit a frame that can comprehend it. The teller's position shapes what the story can hold.

Now jump forward to a modern genre, the narco-narrative, the body of fiction and nonfiction about the drug trade. The most important distinction in this genre is not whether it is fiction or journalism. It is whether the author is from the community being described. An insider, writing from inside the affected community, often adds social context, economic complexity, accountability to that community, and critique from within. That insider voice also carries real danger; some journalists who write from inside have received death threats. An outsider, writing from beyond the community, often reaches wider audiences but misses the texture and context that an insider can see. Same subject, different teller, different truth.

One more example of how the teller builds an argument. In The New Jim Crow, Michelle Alexander does not rest on one decisive proof. She builds through cumulative evidence, a pattern of facts rather than a single proof, so the pattern itself carries the argument. And she applies moral pressure: she holds the United States to its own stated democratic ideals (Alexander, 2010). That is a teller choosing a structure, and the structure shapes what the reader can see.

[PICTURE] Picture three narrators standing in front of the same Andean valley. The first is Garcilaso in 1609, writing in Spanish for readers across an ocean, carefully carrying knowledge that does not quite fit the language he must use. The second is an insider novelist from inside a drug-affected community, seeing the daily texture but writing under threat. The third is an outsider reporter with a wide audience but a narrow view. Same valley, three voices, three different stories, and not one of them is the whole truth.

[REMEMBER] The skill to carry out: the teller shapes the story. Garcilaso's 1609 Comentarios Reales carried Andean knowledge about coca into European conventions, gaining preservation but losing some original context (Garcilaso de la Vega, 1609). In the narco-narrative genre, the decisive question is whether the author is an insider, who adds context, accountability, and critique from within but risks real danger, or an outsider, who reaches wider but misses texture. And Michelle Alexander builds through cumulative evidence and moral pressure, holding the United States to its own ideals (Alexander, 2010). To read any account critically, ask who is telling it, and what their position lets them see and hide.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does it matter whether a narco-narrative is written by an insider or an outsider? [Beat] Because the teller shapes the story: an insider often adds social context, economic complexity, accountability, and critique from within, though at real personal risk, while an outsider reaches wider audiences but misses the texture and context that only someone inside the community can see (Alexander, 2010).

## Key Takeaways
- The teller shapes the story; who narrates determines what the story can show.
- Garcilaso's 1609 Comentarios Reales carried Andean knowledge of coca into European literary conventions, gaining preservation but losing some original context (Garcilaso de la Vega, 1609).
- In narco-narrative, the key distinction is insider versus outsider: insiders add context, accountability, and critique from within but face real danger; outsiders reach wider but miss texture.
- Michelle Alexander builds her argument through cumulative evidence and moral pressure, holding the United States to its own stated ideals (Alexander, 2010).

## Sources
- Garcilaso de la Vega, I. (1609). Comentarios reales de los Incas. (Primary source.)
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.

title: Key Terms: Coca
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 18 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the Coca episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources. If you or someone you know is struggling, the SAMHSA helpline is 1-800-662-4357.

## Geography

**Erythroxylum coca** (eh-RITH-rox-ih-lum KOH-kah). The coca plant, native to the Andean yungas (Plowman, 1984).

**Yungas** (YOON-gahs). The warm, wet Andean foothills at 500 to 2,000 meters where coca grows best.

**Balloon effect.** When eradication pushes drug production to a new area instead of ending it (Dion & Russler, 2008).

**Plan Colombia.** The U.S.-funded eradication program that spent over 10 billion dollars yet saw Colombia's coca double (Dion & Russler, 2008).

## Social Studies

**Acullico** (ah-koo-YEE-koh). The Andean practice of chewing coca leaf with lime, used for at least 4,000 years (Plowman, 1984).

**Cocaine alkaloid.** The active compound in coca, about 0.5 to 1 percent in the leaf but nearly pure in cocaine.

**Crack and powder cocaine.** The same molecule processed differently, but sentenced under a 100 to 1 disparity (Alexander, 2010).

**Coca si, cocaina no.** Bolivia's policy distinguishing the legal traditional leaf from the illegal industrial drug (Grisaffi, 2019).

## Economics

**Cartel** (kar-TEL). A criminal organization controlling drug trafficking in a region, an effect of criminalization (Bowden, 2001).

**Price premium.** The extra cost criminalization adds to a drug, captured by traffickers rather than farmers.

**Mass incarceration.** Large-scale imprisonment driven substantially by the War on Drugs, linked to the racial wealth gap (Alexander, 2010).

## English Language Arts

**Reductive framing.** Simplifying a complex issue to a single dimension, as in 'Just Say No' (Reinarman & Levine, 1997).

**Indigenous sovereignty.** The right of Indigenous peoples to govern their own lands and practices, including coca.

**Moral pressure.** A rhetorical strategy that uses an adversary's stated values against their actions (Alexander, 2010).

**Narco-narrative.** The literary genre about the drug trade, divided by insider versus outsider authors.

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.
- Bowden, M. (2001). Killing Pablo: The hunt for the world's greatest outlaw. Atlantic Monthly Press.
- Dion, M. L., & Russler, C. (2008). Eradication efforts, the state, displacement and poverty. Journal of Latin American Studies, 40(3), 399-421.
- Grisaffi, T. (2019). Coca yes, cocaine no: How Bolivia's coca growers reshaped democracy. Duke University Press.
- Plowman, T. (1984). The ethnobotany of coca (Erythroxylum spp., Erythroxylaceae). Advances in Economic Botany, 1, 62-111.
- Reinarman, C., & Levine, H. G. (1997). Crack in America: Demon drugs and social justice. University of California Press.

title: Cumulative Review: Coca
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the Coca episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. Coca grows in the warm, wet Andean yungas, so production concentrates in Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia, and despite decades of eradication, global cultivation hit a record in 2022 (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023). The reason is the balloon effect: Plan Colombia spent more than 10 billion dollars spraying, yet Colombia's coca more than doubled, because attacking the crop just moves it (Dion & Russler, 2008). And along the supply chain, the farmer earns the least while traffickers capture the value.

Now social studies. The heart of the episode is one distinction: coca leaf is not cocaine. Acullico is mild and 4,000 years old; cocaine is a concentrated 19th-century industrial drug (Plowman, 1984). The crack and powder sentencing gap put Black Americans at about 80 percent of federal crack sentences for the same molecule white Americans mostly used as powder (Alexander, 2010). And Bolivia's "coca si, cocaina no" defends the traditional leaf as Indigenous sovereignty (Grisaffi, 2019).

Then economics. Criminalization built the cartels, creating a price premium, a monopoly, and violence as enforcement, which is how Escobar grew worth about 30 billion dollars (Bowden, 2001). Mass incarceration from the drug war widened the racial wealth gap, while Bolivia's legal model gives farmers income for a fraction of eradication's cost (Alexander, 2010; Grisaffi, 2019).

Last, language. "Just Say No" was reductive framing, and competing frames, moral failure, public health, racial justice, Indigenous sovereignty, and economics, each imply a different policy (Reinarman & Levine, 1997). And who tells the story matters, from Garcilaso de la Vega translating Andean knowledge to the insider voices of narco-narrative (Alexander, 2010).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread. Coca is the season's sharpest case of one plant, two realities: a sacred leaf and an industrial drug, treated as if they were the same. Geography shows eradication failing, history shows the distinction erased and the punishment fell by race, economics shows criminalization enriching cartels and impoverishing communities, and language shows how framing decides the policy. Coca leaf is not cocaine.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. What is the single most important idea of this episode? [Beat] That coca leaf and cocaine are not the same thing; the mild sacred leaf becomes the dangerous drug only through industrial processing (Plowman, 1984).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: coca stays in the Andean yungas, and eradication fails through the balloon effect (United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, 2023; Dion & Russler, 2008).
- Social studies: coca leaf is not cocaine, and the crack/powder disparity fell heavily by race (Plowman, 1984; Alexander, 2010).
- Economics: criminalization built the cartels and mass incarceration widened the racial wealth gap, while Bolivia offers an alternative (Bowden, 2001; Grisaffi, 2019).
- Language: framing from 'Just Say No' to narco-narrative shapes which policy seems obvious (Reinarman & Levine, 1997).

## Sources
- Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow. The New Press.
- Bowden, M. (2001). Killing Pablo. Atlantic Monthly Press.
- Dion, M. L., & Russler, C. (2008). Eradication efforts, the state, displacement and poverty. Journal of Latin American Studies, 40(3), 399-421.
- Grisaffi, T. (2019). Coca yes, cocaine no. Duke University Press.
- Plowman, T. (1984). The ethnobotany of coca (Erythroxylum spp., Erythroxylaceae). Advances in Economic Botany, 1, 62-111.
- Reinarman, C., & Levine, H. G. (1997). Crack in America. University of California Press.
- United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime. (2023). World drug report 2023. https://www.unodc.org

title: Sources and Further Reading: Coca
text_content:
Every claim in this episode is cited inline so you can check it yourself. Here is the full reference list in APA style. Use it to verify a fact, to dig deeper, or to start your own research for the project. The episode is a history and policy class, honest about cocaine's harm, evenhanded on policy, and not an endorsement of use. If you or someone you know is struggling, the SAMHSA national helpline is 1-800-662-4357, free, confidential, and open 24 hours.

## References

Alexander, M. (2010). The new Jim Crow: Mass incarceration in the age of colorblindness. The New Press.

Allen, C. J. (1988). The hold life has: Coca and cultural identity in an Andean community. Smithsonian Institution Press.

Bowden, M. (2001). Killing Pablo: The hunt for the world's greatest outlaw. Atlantic Monthly Press.

Bureau of Justice Statistics. (2023). Prisoners in 2022. U.S. Department of Justice. https://bjs.ojp.gov

Garcilaso de la Vega, I. (1609). Comentarios reales de los Incas.
