title: Knowledge Check: Coca
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 18: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

A reminder before you begin: this is a history and policy class, not an endorsement of use, and cocaine carries serious cardiovascular and addiction risks. Keep the key distinction in mind: coca leaf is not cocaine. If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, the SAMHSA national helpline is free, confidential, and open 24 hours at 1-800-662-4357.

When you finish, take the Project assignment, where the bigger questions, why eradication keeps failing and who bore the cost of the drug war, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"What turns the mild coca leaf into the dangerous drug cocaine?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"Industrial processing that concentrates one compound from about 1 percent up to nearly 100 percent"},{"id":"q1b","text":"Simply drying the leaf"},{"id":"q1c","text":"Chewing it slowly"},{"id":"q1d","text":"Nothing; they are identical"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Cocaine requires industrial chemistry to concentrate the alkaloid from about 1 percent in the leaf to nearly pure, stripping away the cultural context that held the plant in community.","citation":"Lesson: White Lines (intro)"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Where does the coca plant grow best?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"The Andean yungas, the warm, wet foothills at 500 to 2,000 meters"},{"id":"q2b","text":"The high Andean plateau above 5,000 meters"},{"id":"q2c","text":"Dry deserts"},{"id":"q2d","text":"Temperate North America"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"Coca (Erythroxylum coca) needs the warm, wet, frost-free yungas between about 500 and 2,000 meters, which is why it stays in a narrow Andean band.","citation":"Lesson: The Yungas"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Which three countries grow almost all the world's coca?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Colombia (about 67%), Peru (about 26%), and Bolivia (about 7%)"},{"id":"q3b","text":"Mexico, the United States, and Canada"},{"id":"q3c","text":"China, India, and Afghanistan"},{"id":"q3d","text":"France, Spain, and Italy"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"Colombia, Peru, and Bolivia share the Andean yungas geography the plant needs, so coca production concentrates almost entirely in those three countries.","citation":"Lesson: The Yungas"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"What is the balloon effect?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"When eradication in one area pushes drug production to another rather than eliminating it"},{"id":"q4b","text":"A method of smuggling in balloons"},{"id":"q4c","text":"A rise in drug prices"},{"id":"q4d","text":"A farming technique"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Like squeezing a balloon, attacking the crop in one place makes production bulge out elsewhere, so it relocates rather than disappears.","citation":"Lesson: The Balloon Effect"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"What did Plan Colombia's 10 billion dollars and aerial spraying accomplish?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Colombia's coca more than doubled by 2020, while spraying caused environmental and health harm"},{"id":"q5b","text":"It ended coca production entirely"},{"id":"q5c","text":"It made coca safer"},{"id":"q5d","text":"It had no cost"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"After 20 years and over 10 billion dollars, Colombia had more than double its coca, and the glyphosate spraying drifted onto legal crops and waterways until the courts suspended it in 2015.","citation":"Lesson: The Balloon Effect"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"Along the cocaine supply chain, who earns the least and bears the most risk?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"The coca farmer at the start; traffickers in the middle and near the consuming end capture the value"},{"id":"q6b","text":"The consumer"},{"id":"q6c","text":"The trafficking cartels"},{"id":"q6d","text":"Everyone earns equally"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"As in every commodity this series, the grower captures the least and bears the most risk, while the value multiplies toward the traffickers and the consuming end.","citation":"Lesson: Following the Powder"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"What is acullico?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"The Andean practice of chewing coca leaf with lime, a mild stimulant used for at least 4,000 years"},{"id":"q7b","text":"A way to make cocaine"},{"id":"q7c","text":"A Colombian cartel"},{"id":"q7d","text":"A modern energy drink"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Acullico is the traditional chewing of coca leaf with lime, giving a mild effect like coffee, practiced within sacred and communal protocol for thousands of years.","citation":"Lesson: Leaf and Powder"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"How does coca leaf differ from cocaine pharmacologically?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"The leaf has about 0.5 to 1 percent alkaloid and is mild; cocaine is nearly pure, intense, and highly addictive"},{"id":"q8b","text":"They are exactly the same"},{"id":"q8c","text":"The leaf is stronger than cocaine"},{"id":"q8d","text":"Neither has any effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Coca leaf carries roughly 0.5 to 1 percent of the alkaloid and acts like coffee, while cocaine is nearly pure, with intense euphoria, serious heart effects, and high addiction potential.","citation":"Lesson: Leaf and Powder"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"What role did Coca-Cola play in cocaine history?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"It launched in 1886 with coca-leaf extract, before coca was removed from the formula in 1903"},{"id":"q9b","text":"It never contained coca"},{"id":"q9c","text":"It still contains cocaine"},{"id":"q9d","text":"It invented the coca plant"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Coca-Cola launched in 1886 using coca-leaf extract as a tonic, and coca was removed from the formula in 1903 under rising regulatory pressure.","citation":"Lesson: Leaf and Powder"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What was the 1986 crack and powder cocaine sentencing ratio?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"100 to 1: 5 grams of crack triggered the same sentence as 500 grams of powder"},{"id":"q10b","text":"1 to 1; they were treated the same"},{"id":"q10c","text":"Powder was punished 100 times more"},{"id":"q10d","text":"There was no sentencing law"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"The 1986 Anti-Drug Abuse Act set a 100 to 1 ratio: 5 grams of crack carried the same 5-year mandatory minimum as 500 grams of powder, the same molecule treated very differently.","citation":"Lesson: The 100 to 1"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What was the documented racial outcome of the crack/powder disparity?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Black Americans made up roughly 80 percent of federal crack sentences while white Americans were the majority of powder users"},{"id":"q11b","text":"Sentences were equal across races"},{"id":"q11c","text":"White Americans served the most crack sentences"},{"id":"q11d","text":"No one was sentenced"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"The same molecule brought dramatically different consequences by community: Black Americans received about 80 percent of federal crack sentences though white Americans were most powder users and sellers.","citation":"Lesson: The 100 to 1"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"How was the crack/powder disparity partially corrected?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"The 2010 Fair Sentencing Act lowered the ratio to 18 to 1, and the 2018 First Step Act made it retroactive"},{"id":"q12b","text":"It was never changed"},{"id":"q12c","text":"The ratio was raised to 200 to 1"},{"id":"q12d","text":"Crack was legalized"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"The Fair Sentencing Act reduced the ratio from 100 to 1 down to 18 to 1, and the First Step Act made it retroactive, though an 18 to 1 disparity still remains.","citation":"Lesson: The 100 to 1"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"What does Bolivia's 'coca si, cocaina no' policy allow?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"Legal traditional coca cultivation per family, regulated by cocalero cooperatives, while opposing cocaine"},{"id":"q13b","text":"Unlimited cocaine production"},{"id":"q13c","text":"A total ban on the coca leaf"},{"id":"q13d","text":"Foreign control of coca"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Bolivia legalized limited traditional coca cultivation, regulated through grower cooperatives, with markets for tea and chewing, while formally opposing cocaine production.","citation":"Lesson: Coca Si, Cocaina No"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"Who was Evo Morales in the coca story?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"A former coca-growers' union leader who became Bolivia's first Indigenous president in 2005"},{"id":"q14b","text":"An American DEA agent"},{"id":"q14c","text":"A cartel leader"},{"id":"q14d","text":"A coca scientist"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"Morales, a cocalero leader, became Bolivia's first Indigenous president in 2005, expelled the U.S. DEA in 2008, and won a UN treaty carve-out for coca leaf in 2012.","citation":"Lesson: Coca Si, Cocaina No"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"How does criminalization create economic power for trafficking organizations?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"It creates a price premium captured by traffickers, a cartel monopoly, and violence as enforcement"},{"id":"q15b","text":"It lowers all prices"},{"id":"q15c","text":"It gives farmers the profit"},{"id":"q15d","text":"It has no economic effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"Banning cocaine adds a risk premium that traffickers capture, replaces competition with cartels, and makes violence the way to enforce deals, concentrating power and profit.","citation":"Lesson: The Trillion-Dollar War"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"How did Pablo Escobar build his roughly 30 billion dollar fortune?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"On the markup criminalization created, supplying about 80 percent of U.S. cocaine at prices far above production cost"},{"id":"q16b","text":"By farming coca himself"},{"id":"q16c","text":"Through legal business only"},{"id":"q16d","text":"He was not wealthy"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"Escobar's Medellin cartel supplied about 80 percent of U.S. cocaine, and his fortune rested on the gap between low Colombian production cost and high U.S. street price, the markup criminalization creates.","citation":"Lesson: The Trillion-Dollar War"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"How does the War on Drugs spending compare to the cocaine market?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"The U.S. has spent more than 1 trillion dollars since 1971, against a roughly 100 billion dollar yearly cocaine market"},{"id":"q17b","text":"It spent nothing"},{"id":"q17c","text":"It spent less than 1,000 dollars"},{"id":"q17d","text":"The market is larger than all spending"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"U.S. War on Drugs spending has passed 1 trillion dollars since 1971, while the global cocaine market is around 100 billion dollars a year and persists.","citation":"Lesson: The Trillion-Dollar War"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"How does mass incarceration connect to the racial wealth gap?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"It disrupted wealth-building in Black communities during the 1980s to 2000s, a period of broad economic growth"},{"id":"q18b","text":"It increased wealth in those communities"},{"id":"q18c","text":"It had no economic effect"},{"id":"q18d","text":"It only affected the wealthy"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"By removing working-age adults, attaching criminal records, and disenfranchising people, drug-war incarceration disrupted wealth accumulation in Black communities during a key growth period.","citation":"Lesson: The Cost of the Disparity"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What indirect cost does a criminal record impose?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"It restricts access to housing, jobs, and student loans, cutting lifetime earnings"},{"id":"q19b","text":"It raises lifetime earnings"},{"id":"q19c","text":"It has no effect after release"},{"id":"q19d","text":"It guarantees employment"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"A criminal record blocks housing, employment, and student loans, reducing earning capacity long after a sentence ends, one of the documented indirect costs of the disparity.","citation":"Lesson: The Cost of the Disparity"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"How does Bolivia's legal coca model compare to U.S.-funded eradication?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"It gives farmers legal income and keeps communities stable for a fraction of the cost, while eradication destroyed crops and failed to cut production"},{"id":"q20b","text":"Both cost the same and worked"},{"id":"q20c","text":"Eradication was cheaper and effective"},{"id":"q20d","text":"Neither had any effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"Bolivia's domestic model gives farmers 1,000 to 3,000 dollars in legal income and contributes about 350 million dollars to its economy, while Plan Colombia's 10 billion dollars destroyed crops and did not reduce cocaine.","citation":"Lesson: Bolivia's Bet"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"What is reductive framing, as in 'Just Say No'?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"Simplifying a complex issue to a single dimension, here treating drug use as purely an individual moral choice"},{"id":"q21b","text":"Considering every cause at once"},{"id":"q21c","text":"A farming method"},{"id":"q21d","text":"A type of treatment"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Reductive framing collapses a complex problem into one dimension; 'Just Say No' framed addiction as a simple personal choice, implying punishment over treatment or structural change.","citation":"Lesson: Just Say No"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What does each drug-policy frame do, according to the media-literacy lesson?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"It makes some evidence visible and other evidence invisible, and implies a different policy"},{"id":"q22b","text":"Every frame implies the same policy"},{"id":"q22c","text":"Frames have no effect"},{"id":"q22d","text":"Only one frame is allowed"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Whether the frame is moral failure, public health, racial justice, Indigenous sovereignty, or economics, each highlights some facts, hides others, and points to its own policy response.","citation":"Lesson: Just Say No"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What is the most important distinction in narco-narrative writing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"Whether the author is an insider from the community described or an outsider"},{"id":"q23b","text":"Whether it is published in color"},{"id":"q23c","text":"Its length"},{"id":"q23d","text":"Whether it has a happy ending"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"The key distinction is insider versus outsider: insiders add context, complexity, and accountability but risk danger, while outsiders may reach wider audiences but miss texture.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Voice"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"How does Michelle Alexander build her argument in The New Jim Crow?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Through cumulative evidence, a pattern of facts, and moral pressure, holding the U.S. to its own ideals"},{"id":"q24b","text":"With a single decisive proof"},{"id":"q24c","text":"By avoiding evidence"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Through fiction only"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Alexander builds her case from an accumulation of evidence rather than one proof, and uses moral pressure by holding the United States to its stated democratic ideals.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Voice"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"Why does keeping the coca-leaf and cocaine distinction matter?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"Because criminalizing the ancient sacred leaf is a different question from controlling the industrial drug"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Because they are the same and should be treated identically"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Because the leaf is more dangerous"},{"id":"q25d","text":"It does not matter"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"The leaf is a mild, sacred, ancient plant and the drug is a concentrated modern product, so blurring them treats a cultural practice as if it were the destructive drug.","citation":"Lesson: White Lines (intro)"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"Why did global coca cultivation reach a record in 2022?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"Decades of eradication did not reduce supply, because the geography is fixed and demand persistent"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Because eradication was never tried"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Because coca became legal everywhere"},{"id":"q26d","text":"Because demand collapsed"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Despite billions spent on eradication, 2022 saw a record of about 304,000 hectares, showing supply-side enforcement does not overcome stubborn geography and steady demand.","citation":"Lesson: The Yungas"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"Why does Indianapolis appear in the cocaine geography?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"It sits at the crossing of Interstate 65 and 70, a documented Midwest distribution hub"},{"id":"q27b","text":"It grows coca"},{"id":"q27c","text":"It has no role"},{"id":"q27d","text":"It is a coca port"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"At the junction of Interstate 65 and Interstate 70, Indianapolis became a documented Midwest distribution transit point, with the crack era concentrated in specific neighborhoods.","citation":"Lesson: Following the Powder"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"What does the balloon effect reveal about supply-side enforcement?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"Attacking the crop without reducing demand or offering farmers alternatives just relocates production"},{"id":"q28b","text":"It always eliminates the supply"},{"id":"q28c","text":"It lowers demand"},{"id":"q28d","text":"It has no effect on geography"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"Because coca follows profit and weak enforcement, destroying crops in one place pushes growing elsewhere unless demand falls or farmers get another livelihood.","citation":"Lesson: The Balloon Effect"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"What is the public-health frame for drug policy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"Treating addiction as a health condition driven by social factors, implying treatment and harm reduction"},{"id":"q29b","text":"Treating use as a simple moral choice"},{"id":"q29c","text":"Treating drugs as a military enemy"},{"id":"q29d","text":"Ignoring health entirely"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"The public-health frame sees addiction as a health condition shaped by social determinants, pointing toward treatment, prevention, and harm reduction rather than punishment.","citation":"Lesson: Just Say No"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What is the Indigenous-sovereignty frame in the coca debate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"That criminalizing traditional plant use is cultural colonialism, so the plant and the drug should be distinguished"},{"id":"q30b","text":"That all coca should be banned"},{"id":"q30c","text":"That coca has no cultural meaning"},{"id":"q30d","text":"That cocaine should be legal"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"This frame, voiced by Bolivia's cocalero movement, argues that banning the sacred leaf itself is cultural colonialism and that policy should separate the traditional plant from the industrial drug.","citation":"Lesson: Just Say No"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"Who was Inca Garcilaso de la Vega?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"A writer of mixed Inca and Spanish heritage who presented Andean knowledge, including about coca, to a European audience in 1609"},{"id":"q31b","text":"A modern cartel leader"},{"id":"q31c","text":"A U.S. president"},{"id":"q31d","text":"A coca scientist"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Garcilaso, of mixed Inca and Spanish heritage, wrote the 1609 Comentarios Reales, raising the question of what is gained and lost when Andean oral knowledge is translated for Europe.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Voice"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"Why is the same molecule treated so differently as crack and powder?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"Crack and powder are the same drug processed differently, but were sentenced far more harshly in the communities that used crack"},{"id":"q32b","text":"They are different molecules"},{"id":"q32c","text":"Powder was punished more"},{"id":"q32d","text":"There was no difference in sentencing"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"Crack is cocaine made smokable with baking soda, the same molecule as powder, yet the 100 to 1 law fell far more heavily on the largely Black communities where crack was used.","citation":"Lesson: The 100 to 1"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What is the media-literacy skill for reading drug-policy language?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"Asking which frame is being used and who gains from it"},{"id":"q33b","text":"Believing the loudest slogan"},{"id":"q33c","text":"Ignoring all framing"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Memorizing one policy"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Because each frame implies a policy and serves an interest, the key skill is identifying which frame a message uses and who benefits from that framing.","citation":"Lesson: Just Say No"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"What do insider narco-narratives add, and what do they risk?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"They add context and accountability to the community, but their authors can face real danger"},{"id":"q34b","text":"They add nothing and risk nothing"},{"id":"q34c","text":"They only entertain"},{"id":"q34d","text":"They are always fiction"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Insiders bring social and economic complexity and accountability to the depicted community, but some journalists who investigate from within have received death threats.","citation":"Lesson: Whose Voice"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"Where does the coca farmer sit in the trillion-dollar cocaine economy?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"At the bottom, earning a tiny fraction while the markup from criminalization flows to traffickers"},{"id":"q35b","text":"At the top, capturing most profit"},{"id":"q35c","text":"Outside the economy entirely"},{"id":"q35d","text":"Sharing profit equally with cartels"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"The grower earns little of cocaine's value, since the enormous markup created by criminalization is captured by the trafficking organizations, not the farmer.","citation":"Lesson: The Trillion-Dollar War"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"If you or someone you know is struggling with substance use, what resource does this episode give?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"The SAMHSA national helpline, free and confidential 24/7 at 1-800-662-4357"},{"id":"q36b","text":"No resources exist"},{"id":"q36c","text":"A cartel hotline"},{"id":"q36d","text":"Only a website"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Every Season 3 episode points to the SAMHSA national helpline, 1-800-662-4357, free, confidential, and open 24 hours, because cocaine carries serious cardiovascular and addiction risks.","citation":"Lesson: White Lines (intro)"}]}
